topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt force of a thrown brick. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, and the headline screamed my name, but the byline belonged to some stranger. My carefully crafted analysis, the one I’d poured weeks of sleepless nights into, was now attributed to 'A. Sterling.' A. Sterling? I reread the text, the words swimming before my eyes, a slow, burning heat rising up my neck. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. I stood up, pacing the worn rug, my jaw tight enough to crack enamel. I needed to find the editor, needed to confront this absurdity, but the anger was a cold, paralyzing weight, making my limbs feel sluggish and heavy.

[story 2]

When I saw the proof, the color drained from my face. It was a betrayal so profound it felt physical, like a punch to the solar plexus. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid. The name on the masthead was a complete fabrication, a ghost claiming my intellectual property. I stared at the screen, my knuckles white where I gripped the mouse. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat—a sound that was more frustration than actual speech. I snatched my phone, fingers trembling as I scrolled through the publication’s archives, searching for any trace of my contribution, any hint of the real author. Every search result was a dead end, each dead end fueling a simmering, furious resentment. I felt a sudden, desperate need to scream, to tear the whole publication down, but I swallowed the impulse, forcing myself into a rigid stillness, channeling the rising tide of sheer outrage into a tense, controlled stillness.

[story 3]

I found the link tucked away in a forgotten corner of the website’s backend—a ghost file, a digital breadcrumb leading straight to the published piece. My hands started shaking so badly I had to press them against my thighs to steady them. When the true author’s name appeared, a sharp, almost painful clenching seized my chest. I let out a short, sharp laugh, devoid of any humor. It was a brittle sound, like glass shattering. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city traffic, feeling a profound sense of unfairness settle over me, heavy and suffocating. I turned back to the computer, not with a plan, but with a sudden, sharp resolve. I began typing, not an apology, but a meticulously worded rebuttal, each sentence calibrated to cut precisely where the deception had occurred.
